eurasian-treecreeper.region: Found across the Palearctic in woodlands of all kinds, from British Isles through Europe to Asia; where it overlaps with a close relative in western Europe, it favours coniferous forests and higher altitudes.
eurasian-treecreeper.behavior: It spirals up tree trunks like a mouse, probing bark crevices with a fine curved bill to extract insects, then drops to the base of the next tree in an erratic, undulating flight.
eurasian-treecreeper.funFact: The Eurasian Treecreeper sometimes roosts communally on cold nights, pressing several birds together into a huddle on a sheltered patch of bark to conserve warmth.

winter-wren.region: This species breeds in coniferous forests stretching from British Columbia to the Atlantic coast, then migrates south to spend the colder season across the eastern United States and southeastern Canada.
winter-wren.behavior: It forages almost exclusively by ground-gleaning in dense underbrush, picking through leaf litter and low tangles for small invertebrates; its genus name means 'cave-dweller', reflecting its habit of creeping into cavities and thick cover.
winter-wren.funFact: Despite being one of the smallest birds on the continent, the Winter Wren produces a remarkably loud, complex song — its vocal output relative to body weight is among the highest of any bird.

demoiselle-crane.region: This species ranges across central Eurosiberia from the Black Sea to Mongolia and northeast China, with a small population in Turkey; western birds migrate to Africa for the cold months, while Asian populations fly to the Indian subcontinent.
demoiselle-crane.behavior: A long-distance migrant, it travels in large flocks and must cross the Himalayas — one of the most gruelling migration routes of any bird; it feeds on seeds, grains, and small invertebrates on open grasslands and agricultural fields.
demoiselle-crane.funFact: The Demoiselle Crane is the smallest crane species in the world, yet its Himalayan crossing takes it above 5,000 metres, where it must contend with extreme cold and low oxygen.

black-headed-ibis.region: This wading species breeds across South and Southeast Asia, from India westward to as far east as Japan, frequenting wetlands, rice paddies, and a variety of other natural and man-made habitats.
black-headed-ibis.behavior: It uses its long, down-curved black bill to probe shallow water and soft soil for fish, frogs, crustaceans, and insects; it nests colonially but only during the rainy season.
black-headed-ibis.funFact: The Black-headed Ibis is the only native wading species in its South Asian range with all-white plumage contrasted by a bare black head and neck — a combination that makes it unmistakable at a glance.

indian-white-eye.region: A resident breeder of open woodland and gardens across the subcontinent and into South Asia, this small passerine is rarely found far from its core range following a recent taxonomic split that separated it from related Southeast Asian populations.
indian-white-eye.behavior: It forages in small, active flocks, feeding on nectar — which it sips with a brush-tipped tongue — as well as small insects gleaned from foliage; the distinctive pale eye-ring is visible even at a distance.
indian-white-eye.funFact: The Indian White-eye was only recently given its current name after genetic studies split the former 'Oriental white-eye' into multiple species, making this bird the sole recognised representative of its lineage on the subcontinent.

pheasant-tailed-jacana.region: Found across tropical Asia from Yemen in the west to the Philippines in the east, this species inhabits shallow lakes and wetlands thick with floating vegetation; it undertakes long seasonal migrations — unique among its family.
pheasant-tailed-jacana.behavior: Extraordinarily elongated toes spread its weight so it can walk atop lily pads and other floating plants while foraging for invertebrates; females are larger than males and are polyandrous, leaving each clutch of eggs to be incubated and raised by a different male.
pheasant-tailed-jacana.funFact: The Pheasant-tailed Jacana undergoes a dramatic seasonal transformation: its striking long tail and bold chestnut-and-white breeding plumage are replaced by a much plainer non-breeding appearance after the nesting season ends.
